title: Woven curtain panel on a rigid heddle loom
subtitle: A lightweight plain-weave curtain panel in cotton or linen, woven on a rigid heddle loom and hung with clip rings
excerpt: A single curtain panel is a practical functional weaving project that demonstrates how a rigid heddle loom can produce real cloth for real use. This entry covers calculating the warp for a finished panel of 60 × 90 cm, weaving at the correct sett, and finishing the edges so the panel hangs evenly.
sourceNotes: Rigid heddle curtain panel construction synthesised from general rigid heddle weaving practice. Cotton and linen warp and weft in plain weave at a standard sett produces a functional sheer or light curtain panel.

glossaryTerms.definition: The set of threads running lengthways through the loom, on which the weft interlaces to produce the cloth. For a curtain panel, a strong cotton or linen warp resists the repeated stress of hanging.
The thread carried horizontally across the warp on each pick. For a sheer curtain, the weft is often the same yarn as the warp; for a lighter, more open panel, the weft may be finer.
The number of warp ends per centimetre. For a semi-transparent curtain panel in 4-ply cotton, a sett of 8-9 ends per cm produces a stable but open cloth.
A finishing stitch worked along the cut warp ends of a woven piece, wrapping groups of warp ends together to secure the weft and prevent unravelling.

content.text: A curtain panel is one of the most practical rigid heddle projects. It needs no complex threading, uses cotton or linen yarn, and produces cloth you will use every day. The main decisions are choosing the right
for the desired transparency, leaving enough allowance for shrinkage, and finishing the cut ends so the
does not unravel at the top and bottom edges.

items.name: Cotton or linen yarn, 4-ply or DK weight
Rigid heddle loom, at least 65 cm weaving width

content.text: Allow 15% for loom shrinkage on the length and 10% for width draw-in. For a finished panel of 60 cm wide × 90 cm long, wind the warp to 67 cm wide in the heddle and 120 cm long (plus 40 cm loom waste). At 8 ends per cm, the warp width of 67 cm requires 536 ends. Use a smooth, strong cotton or linen: the curtain will carry its own weight when hung, and a weak warp end will break at the clip ring attachment.
Step 2: Thread and dress the loom

Thread alternately through slots and holes across the full heddle width. Beam the warp under firm, even tension. Tie the front ends to the front beam in bundles of 8-10 ends, adjusting until the surface is level. A level
is impossible to produce on an uneven warp surface: spend time at this stage getting the tension right.

Weave in plain weave, letting the weft arc across the shed before each beat. Count the picks per centimetre in the first 5 cm and maintain the same rate for the full length. Consistent beat produces an even cloth with consistent shrinkage across its height. Aim for a weft-packing density that makes the cloth feel firm but light when held up to the window: a curtain should have some transparency.
Cut the panel from the loom leaving 4-5 cm of warp fringe at each end. Work

along both cut edges using a blunt darning needle and the same yarn as the warp: wrap groups of 4-6 warp ends together in bundles, pulling the stitch firmly to compress the last weft pick against the cut ends. Trim the fringe to 1 cm after stitching, or fold and slip-stitch the hem to the back. Wash by hand in cool water, press flat while damp, and hang with clip rings evenly spaced across the top hem.

items.problem: The panel is narrower at the bottom than the top after washing
items.cause: The weft tension was inconsistent: the cloth was woven more tightly at the beginning of the warp
items.fix: Check selvedge width every 10 picks throughout weaving. The width in the weaving should match the width in the reed. Consistent arc and consistent beat are the two variables to control.

items.problem: The warp fringe unravels at the hem-stitched edge
items.cause: The hem stitch was worked too loosely and did not compress the last weft pick against the cut ends
items.fix: Re-work the hem stitch, pulling each stitch firmly so the warp bundles grip the last weft pick. Apply a small amount of clear fabric glue along the cut edge before trimming if the yarn is particularly slippery.

items.problem: The cloth is completely opaque rather than semi-transparent
items.cause: The sett is too close for the yarn weight, or the weft was beaten too firmly
items.fix: For a more open cloth, re-warp using a heddle with larger slot spacing, or use a finer yarn in the weft only while keeping the warp at the same sett.
